name: Polygon.io
description_short: Polygon.io pricing, free tier limits, API features, and real-time market data plans compared for developers, algo traders, and fintech builders.
description_long:
## Polygon.io Pricing: Quick Verdict

Polygon.io is one of the strongest market data APIs for developers who need clean REST endpoints, WebSocket streaming, historical bars, reference data, and production-grade stock market data. The key pricing question is simple: delayed data is cheap, but real-time stock data starts at the Advanced tier.

For most traders and builders, the practical decision is between the free Basic plan for testing, the $29/month Starter plan for delayed data with unlimited API calls, the $79/month Developer plan for deeper historical access, and the $199/month Advanced plan for real-time stock data. That makes Polygon.io much more serious than a casual charting tool. It is best judged as data infrastructure.

## Free Tier And Rate Limits

Polygon.io's free Basic stock plan is useful for learning the API and testing a small prototype, but it is not enough for a production scanner or trading app. The main limit is the 5 API calls per minute cap. That is fine for one-off requests, examples, and small tests, but it becomes restrictive as soon as you pull data for a watchlist or run scheduled jobs.

The free tier is still valuable because it lets developers test the response format, endpoint behavior, authentication, and data model before paying. If you are comparing market data APIs, this matters. You can validate whether Polygon.io fits your stack before committing to a $29, $79, or $199 monthly plan.

## Paid Plans: Starter, Developer, And Advanced

The paid stock plans change the product from a demo API into a serious data feed. Starter at $29/month is the first real upgrade because it removes the tiny free-tier request ceiling and gives delayed market data for builders who do not need live prices. Developer at $79/month is better for larger historical research and more demanding backtesting workflows.

Advanced at $199/month is the major pricing cliff because this is where real-time stock data becomes the core value. If you are building alerts, dashboards, automated trading infrastructure, or a fintech product where delayed quotes are unacceptable, this is the plan most people end up evaluating.

## What Polygon.io Is Best At

Polygon.io is strongest when the user is technical. Developers get REST APIs, WebSockets, standardized JSON and CSV formats, historical aggregates, reference data, fundamentals, corporate actions, and market coverage across stocks, options, forex, and crypto. It is a builder's tool, not a point-and-click trading terminal.

That distinction is important. A discretionary trader who wants charts and watchlists may be better served by TradingView, Koyfin, or a broker platform. A developer building a scanner, dashboard, research app, or automated workflow is closer to Polygon.io's ideal customer.

## Polygon.io vs Alpha Vantage

Alpha Vantage is easier and cheaper for simple prototypes, especially if you want technical indicators through simple REST calls. Polygon.io is the stronger option once you need more reliable production infrastructure, richer stock data, WebSocket streaming, and real-time access. The tradeoff is price. Alpha Vantage is easier to start with; Polygon.io is easier to grow into.

If you only need a few end-of-day pulls or a classroom project, Alpha Vantage may be enough. If your app needs real-time market data, reliable historical coverage, and a more developer-focused data platform, Polygon.io is the more serious foundation.

## Hidden Costs And Practical Gotchas

The biggest gotcha is not a surprise fee. It is plan fit. Users often start by looking for "Polygon.io pricing" and then discover that the plan they actually need depends on latency, asset class, history depth, and whether they need WebSockets. Real-time data costs more than delayed data, and options or multi-asset use cases can change the budget quickly.

Another practical issue is that Polygon.io is infrastructure. You still need to build the user interface, database, alerts, trading logic, or analytics layer yourself. If you are not technical, the API may feel powerful but abstract.

## Bottom Line

Polygon.io deserves a high rating because it solves a hard problem well: reliable financial market data for developers. The free tier is good for testing, the $29 and $79 tiers are useful for delayed-data workflows, and the $199 Advanced plan is the realistic entry point for real-time stock data.

Choose Polygon.io if you are building software around market data. Choose a charting platform if you mainly want to look at charts. For developers, algo traders, and fintech builders, Polygon.io remains one of the best market data APIs to evaluate in 2026.
